summary: Warren Buffett outlines Berkshire Hathaway's core economic principles of operation, emphasizing a long-term partnership attitude with shareholders, the primacy of intrinsic per-share value over corporate size, conservative use of debt, and extreme candor in financial reporting. He also details his decentralized management style and the critical distinction between accounting book value and true intrinsic value.

principles.statement: Although our form is corporate, our attitude is partnership.
principles.rationale: Shareholders should be viewed as owner-partners and the company merely as a conduit through which they own the assets, fostering a long-term, co-venturer relationship.
principles.source_quote: Although our form is corporate, our attitude is partnership. Charlie Munger and I think of our shareholders as owner-partners, and of ourselves as managing partners.

principles.statement: Maximize average annual rate of gain in intrinsic business value on a per-share basis.
principles.rationale: Economic significance and performance should be measured by per-share progress, not by the overall size of the company's balance sheet.
principles.source_quote: Our long-term economic goal (subject to some qualifications mentioned later) is to maximize Berkshire’s average annual rate of gain in intrinsic business value on a per-share basis. We do not measure the economic significance or performance of Berkshire by its size; we measure by per-share progress.

principles.statement: Accounting consequences do not influence our operating or capital-allocation decisions.
principles.rationale: Standard accounting principles often obscure true economic performance. It is better to acquire unreportable but higher actual earnings than lower reportable earnings.
principles.source_quote: When acquisition costs are similar, we much prefer to purchase $2 of earnings that is not reportable by us under standard accounting principles than to purchase $1 of earnings that is reportable.

principles.statement: We use debt sparingly and will reject interesting opportunities rather than over-leverage our balance sheet.
principles.rationale: Fiduciary obligations to policyholders, lenders, and equity holders demand conservatism. Risking what you have and need for what you don't have and don't need is unacceptable.
principles.source_quote: The financial calculus that Charlie and I employ would never permit our trading a good night’s sleep for a shot at a few extra percentage points of return.

principles.statement: We will issue common stock only when we receive as much in business value as we give.
principles.rationale: Issuing shares is equivalent to selling a portion of the company. Selling assets for less than their intrinsic value unfairly penalizes existing owners.
principles.source_quote: We will not sell small portions of your company – and that is what the issuance of shares amounts to – on a basis inconsistent with the value of the entire enterprise.

principles.statement: Be candid in reporting, emphasizing both the pluses and minuses important in appraising business value.
principles.rationale: Shareholders deserve the same facts managers would want if their positions were reversed. Furthermore, misleading others publicly leads to misleading oneself privately.
principles.source_quote: Our guideline is to tell you the business facts that we would want to know if our positions were reversed. We owe you no less.

frameworks.name: The Retained Earnings Test
frameworks.when_to_apply: When evaluating whether to retain earnings within the business or distribute them to shareholders.
steps: Evaluate performance on a five-year rolling basis.
Check if the book-value gain exceeded the performance of the S&P 500 during that period.
Check if the stock consistently sold at a premium to book value, proving that every $1 retained was valued by the market at more than $1.
frameworks.source_quote: The five-year test should be: (1) during the period did our book-value gain exceed the performance of the S&P; and (2) did our stock consistently sell at a premium to book, meaning that every $1 of retained earnings was always worth more than $1?

mental_models.name: The Sinking Market as a Supermarket
mental_models.description: Viewing a depressed stock market not as a cause for panic, but as a discount mechanism that benefits net buyers of businesses and stocks.
mental_models.example: Benefiting from a sinking stock market much as a regular purchaser of food benefits from declining food prices.

mental_models.name: Intrinsic Value vs. Book Value (The College Education Analogy)
mental_models.description: Book value is the historical cost of an asset, while intrinsic value is the discounted value of the cash that can be taken out of it over its remaining life. They are rarely the same.
mental_models.example: The 'book value' of a college education is its cost plus foregone earnings. Its 'intrinsic value' is the discounted excess earnings the graduate will receive over their lifetime compared to what they would have earned without it.

mental_models.name: Look-Through Earnings
mental_models.description: Evaluating a company's true operating gain by including the undistributed earnings of its investees, rather than just the dividends officially distributed and reported under standard accounting.

heuristics: To finish first, you must first finish.
We will only do with your money what we would do with our own.
We delegate almost to the point of abdication.

signature_quotes.text: Although our form is corporate, our attitude is partnership.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the foundational relationship between Berkshire's management and its shareholders.

signature_quotes.text: The financial calculus that Charlie and I employ would never permit our trading a good night’s sleep for a shot at a few extra percentage points of return.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining Berkshire's extreme conservatism regarding the use of debt.

signature_quotes.text: I’ve never believed in risking what my family and friends have and need in order to pursue what they don’t have and don’t need.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the rationale for avoiding over-leverage.

signature_quotes.text: Overall, Berkshire and its long-term shareholders benefit from a sinking stock market much as a regular purchaser of food benefits from declining food prices.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why shareholders should neither panic nor mourn when the market plummets.

signature_quotes.text: The CEO who misleads others in public may eventually mislead himself in private.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why candor in financial reporting is beneficial to managers as well as shareholders.

signature_quotes.text: Intrinsic value can be defined simply: It is the discounted value of the cash that can be taken out of a business during its remaining life.
signature_quotes.context: Defining the core metric used to evaluate investments.

anti_patterns.description: Gin rummy managerial behavior (discarding your least promising business at each turn).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It treats businesses and their employees as disposable commodities rather than long-term commitments, undermining trust and stability.

anti_patterns.description: Issuing undervalued stock to raise capital or make acquisitions.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Owners unfairly lose if their managers deliberately sell assets for 80¢ that in fact are worth $1. It destroys per-share intrinsic value for existing shareholders.

anti_patterns.description: Big bath accounting maneuvers or 'smoothing' of quarterly/annual results.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It obscures the true economic reality of the business, misleading shareholders and eventually the managers themselves.

anti_patterns.description: Providing earnings 'guidance' to analysts or large shareholders.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It gives an unfair edge to select individuals, violating the principle that all owners should be updated at the same time.
